content.problemStatement:
A permutation of length NNN is an array of length NNN that contains every integer from 111 to NNN exactly once.

For example, [1,3,2][1, 3, 2][1,3,2] and [2,1,3][2, 1, 3][2,1,3] are permutations of length 333, but [2,1,2][2, 1, 2][2,1,2] and [3,4,2][3, 4, 2][3,4,2] are not.

The beauty of a permutation PPP is defined to be the number of prefixes of PPP that are themselves permutations.

That is, it is the number of integers iii (1≤i≤N1 \leq i \leq N1≤i≤N) such that the array [P1,P2,…,Pi][P_1, P_2, \ldots, P_i][P1​,P2​,…,Pi​] is a permutation of length iii.

You are given a permutation PPP of length NNN.

You can perform the following operation on it **at most once**:


Choose two integers LLL and RRR such that 1≤L<R≤N1 \leq L \lt R \leq N1≤L<R≤N, and LLL and RRR have different parities.

That is, if LLL is odd then RRR should be even, and vice versa.


Then, for each i=L,L+2,L+4,…,R−1i = L, L+2, L+4, \ldots, R-1i=L,L+2,L+4,…,R−1, swap PiP_iPi​ with Pi+1P_{i+1}Pi+1​.
In simpler words, you can choose a subarray of PPP that has even length, partition it into blocks of length 222, and swap the elements within each block.

For example, if P=[3,1,5,2,6,4]P = [3, 1, 5, 2, 6, 4]P=[3,1,5,2,6,4] and you choose L=2L = 2L=2 and R=5R = 5R=5, the resulting permutation is P=[3,5,1,6,2,4]P = [3, 5, 1, 6, 2, 4]P=[3,5,1,6,2,4].

Find the **maximum** possible beauty of PPP after performing at most one such operation.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the length of the permutation.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers P1,P2,…,PNP_1, P_2, \ldots, P_NP1​,P2​,…,PN​.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the maximum possible beauty of PPP after performing at most one operation.
constraints: PPP contains each integer from 111 to NNN exactly once.
The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Perform the operation with L=1,R=4L = 1, R = 4L=1,R=4 to obtain [1,3,2,4,5][1, 3, 2, 4, 5][1,3,2,4,5].
The prefixes of length 1,3,4,51, 3, 4, 51,3,4,5 are permutations now, which is the best we can do.
Test case 222: Perform the operation with L=5,R=6L = 5, R = 6L=5,R=6 to obtain [2,1,3,4,5,6][2, 1, 3, 4, 5, 6][2,1,3,4,5,6] which has every prefix except length 111 be a permutation.
Test case 333: It's optimal to not perform any operation, every prefix is already a permutation.
